title.emphasis: Section II: Explicit Affirmations in State Constitutions

section.para: In the post-Dobbs legal environment, the most powerful and durable affirmations of bodily autonomy are those enshrined within state constitutions. These protections arise from two distinct sources: direct constitutional amendments passed by voters, a trend that has accelerated significantly since 2022, and pre-existing constitutional provisions that state supreme courts have interpreted to protect these rights. Together, they form the bedrock of legally recognized bodily autonomy in the United States.

title.emphasis: Post-Dobbs Constitutional Amendments: A Wave of Direct Democracy

section.para: The most direct and explicit response to the Dobbs decision has been a wave of citizen-led ballot initiatives to amend state constitutions, enshrining protections for reproductive rights and autonomy directly into the states' foundational legal documents.15 This exercise of direct democracy represents the strongest possible affirmation of bodily autonomy at the state level. Since June 2022, voters in eleven states have approved such measures:

listitem.para: California (2022): Proposition 1 amended the state constitution to explicitly establish a "fundamental right to choose to have an abortion" and a "fundamental right to choose or refuse contraceptives".15

Michigan (2022): Proposal 3 established a "fundamental right to reproductive freedom," which is broadly defined to include decisions about prenatal care, childbirth, postpartum care, contraception, sterilization, abortion care, miscarriage management, and infertility care.15
Vermont (2022): Article 22 was added to the state constitution, establishing a right to "personal reproductive autonomy".15

Ohio (2023): Issue 1 amended the constitution to protect an individual's right to "make and carry out one's own reproductive decisions," including but not limited to decisions about abortion, contraception, fertility treatment, and miscarriage care.16

Arizona (2024): Proposition 139 established a "fundamental right to an abortion" up to the point of fetal viability and thereafter when necessary to protect the life or health of the pregnant person.16

Colorado (2024): Amendment 89 recognized a "right to abortion" in the state constitution and repealed a previous constitutional provision that prohibited the use of state funds for abortion coverage.16
Maryland (2024): Question 1 guaranteed a "fundamental right to reproductive freedom," including the ability to prevent, continue, or end one's own pregnancy.16
Missouri (2024): Amendment 3 protected "reproductive freedom" and the ability to make and effectuate decisions about reproductive health, including abortion up to viability.16
Montana (2024): CI-128 affirmed a "right to make and carry out decisions about one's own pregnancy, including the right to abortion".16

Nevada (2024): Question 6 established a "fundamental right to abortion" until fetal viability. Under Nevada law, this measure must be approved by voters again in the 2026 general election before the amendment is added to the constitution.16
New York (2024): Proposal 1 amended the state's equal rights provision to prohibit discrimination based on "pregnancy, pregnancy outcomes, and reproductive healthcare and autonomy".16

section.para: The varied language used in these amendments is not merely semantic; it reflects a deliberate and forward-looking legal strategy. The choice of broader terms like "reproductive freedom" (Michigan, Maryland, Missouri) and "reproductive autonomy" (Vermont, New York) over the more specific "right to abortion" creates a more flexible and powerful constitutional foundation. A right to "reproductive autonomy," for instance, can be litigated to protect not only abortion but also access to contraception, fertility treatments like in-vitro fertilization (IVF), and gender-affirming care, thereby preempting future legislative attacks on a wider range of bodily autonomy issues.4 This suggests that the legal battles of the next decade in these states will focus on defining the full scope and application of these new, broadly-worded rights.

row.entry: "fundamental right to choose to have an abortion" and "fundamental right to choose or refuse contraceptives"
"right to make and carry out one's own reproductive decisions"
Md. Const. Decl. of Rights, art. 49
"right to make and carry out decisions about one's own pregnancy"
Prohibits discrimination based on "reproductive healthcare and autonomy"

title.emphasis: Judicial Interpretations of Existing Constitutional Rights

section.para: In a second category of states, protections for bodily autonomy have been affirmed not by new amendments but by state supreme court interpretations of pre-existing constitutional text. State courts, as the final arbiters of their own constitutions, can interpret them to provide greater protections than the U.S. Constitution, and several have done so by grounding abortion rights in unique state provisions concerning privacy or natural rights.18

listitem.para: Alaska: The Alaska Constitution contains an explicit right to privacy, added in 1972, which states: "The right of the people to privacy is recognized and shall not be infringed" (Art. I, § 22).28 In the landmark case Valley Hospital Ass'n v. Mat-Su Coalition (1997), the Alaska Supreme Court held that this provision is more protective than the federal constitution and encompasses a woman's fundamental right to make reproductive choices, including the decision to have an abortion.18 This precedent remains binding law in Alaska and has been reaffirmed by lower courts in the state post- Dobbs.31

Kansas: The Kansas Supreme Court, in its 2019 decision Hodes & Nauser v. Schmidt, found a fundamental right to bodily autonomy within Section 1 of the Kansas Constitution Bill of Rights.18 This section, which echoes the Declaration of Independence, states: "All men are possessed of equal and inalienable natural rights, among which are life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness".33 The court ruled that this "natural rights guarantee" protects the right of personal autonomy, which includes the ability to control one's own body and to decide whether to continue a pregnancy.36

Montana: The Montana Constitution contains one of the strongest explicit privacy clauses in the nation. Article II, Section 10 states: "The right of individual privacy is essential to the well-being of a free society and shall not be infringed without the showing of a compelling state interest".39 In Armstrong v. State (1999), the Montana Supreme Court held that this provision broadly guarantees "the right to make medical judgments affecting her or his bodily integrity and health in partnership with a chosen health care provider," and that this includes a woman's right of "procreative autonomy".18

Minnesota: In its 1995 decision in Doe v. Gomez, the Minnesota Supreme Court found that the right of privacy under the Minnesota Constitution, derived from the liberty protections in its Bill of Rights, is more expansive than the federal right and encompasses a woman's decision to terminate a pregnancy.18 While the state constitution does not have a standalone privacy clause, the court inferred the right from its broader protections for individual liberty.47

section.para: These judicial interpretations, however, exist on a different tier of durability than direct constitutional amendments. The 2024 reversal by the Florida Supreme Court serves as a stark cautionary tale. For decades, beginning with In re T.W. (1989), the court had interpreted Florida's explicit constitutional right to privacy—"Every natural person has the right to be let alone and free from governmental intrusion into the person's private life" (Art. I, § 23)—as protecting abortion rights.27 In April 2024, a court with a different ideological composition overturned this long-standing precedent, ruling that the privacy clause no longer protects abortion rights.50 This action, which directly parallels the U.S. Supreme Court's overturning of

Roe, demonstrates that a right granted by judicial interpretation can be taken away by a later court. In contrast, a right enshrined in the constitutional text by a direct vote of the people can only be removed by another constitutional amendment, a significantly higher political and legal barrier.20 This distinction creates two tiers of constitutional protection: the robust and durable rights in states with recent amendments, and the strong but inherently more fragile rights in states that rely on judicial precedent.

row.entry: The explicit right to privacy in the Alaska Constitution is more expansive than federal protections and encompasses a woman's fundamental right to make reproductive choices.
The "inalienable natural rights" of life and liberty protect a fundamental right of personal autonomy, which includes the ability to control one's own body and decide whether to continue a pregnancy.
The explicit right of individual privacy guarantees the right to make medical judgments affecting one's bodily integrity and health, which includes a right to "procreative autonomy."
The right of privacy under the Minnesota Constitution is more expansive than the federal right and encompasses a woman's right to decide to terminate her pregnancy.
*Original Holding:* The explicit right to privacy protected a woman's decision whether or not to continue her pregnancy. *Current Status:* This precedent was overturned in 2024.
